Want to Stay and Work in Taiwan After Graduation? 2026 New Policy: 2-Year Open Work Period Without a Work Permit

  • Post published:2026 年 3 月 30 日
  • Post category:Job Search Strategy / Work Regulation

Written by 104 Foreigners

As Taiwan continues to attract international talent, the government has introduced more flexible policies for International & Overseas Chinese students students. Starting from 2026, eligible graduates can benefit from a more flexible job-seeking system, including a 2-year open work period without requiring a work permit. If you are about to graduate or planning to start your career in Taiwan, this guide will help you understand: the 2-year open work period policy, how to apply for an Extension of Stay, eligibility for the work permit exemption, preparation to stay in Taiwan after graduation and key steps to transition into long-term employment.

Table of Contents (Click to quickly navigate)

  • 2-Year Open Work Period After Graduation
  • Who Is Eligible for the Work Permit Exemption?
  • How to Apply for an “Extension of Stay”
  • After the Exemption Period: How to Continue Working in Taiwan
  • Must Know How Can International Graduates Start Preparing for Employment in Taiwan?
  • 2026 New Policy: Gain Practical Experience and Explore Your Career Path
  • Must Know FAQ for International and Overseas Chinese Graduates

2-Year Open Work Period After Graduation

To provide graduates with more time to plan their future careers, Taiwan government offers eligible a flexible job-search period of up to two years after graduation, helping overseas Chinese and international graduates explore suitable job opportunities in Taiwan with peace of mind. Under the 2026 policy, graduates may apply for an “Extension of Stay” through the National Immigration Agency (NIA) .

  • International & Overseas Chinese students: 1 year + 1 year
  • Hong Kong & Macao students: 6 months + 6 months

During this period, no work permit is required, thus employers do NOT need to apply for a work permit on your behalf. You are also permitted to work flexibly, including full-time or part-time employment, internships, and project-based or freelance work, without restrictions on industry or job type. This period is designed to reduce pressure to secure a job immediately after graduation, allow exploration of different career paths, and help you gain practical work experience in Taiwan.

Who Is Eligible for the Work Permit Exemption?

To qualify, you must meet all of the following:

  • Be an overseas Chinese student or international student
  • Hold at least an associate degree or higher from a Taiwanese institution
  • Obtain an “Extension of Stay” status after graduation

Additional conditions:

  • Graduates from 2026 onward:
    Eligible during the “Extension of Stay” period
  • Graduates before 2026:
    Must be within 2 years after graduation and have never obtained a formal work permit previously

How to Apply for an “Extension of Stay”

If you plan to stay in Taiwan after graduation to look for a job, you must apply for an “Extension of Stay” before your current Alien Resident Certificate (ARC) expires. You are required to apply in person at a local service center of the National Immigration Agency (NIA) within 30 days before your ARC expires. It is recommended to prepare your documents in advance and submit your application within 15 days after receiving your graduation certificate, to avoid any interruption to your residency status.

Required documents:

  1. Application form
  2. Passport (valid for at least 6 months)
  3. Graduation certificate
  4. Current ARC
  5. 2-inch color ID photo
  6. Rental agreement (if address changed)

Application Fee: NT$1,000

Processing time: around 10 working days. Once notified, you can collect your new residence permit from the NIA

Validity:

  • International & Overseas Chinese students: 1 year per issuance (max. 2 years)
  • Hongkong & Macao students: 6 months per issuance (max. 1 year)

You must apply for an extension before each period expires if you wish to continue staying in Taiwan.

Note: This period does NOT count toward permanent residency (APRC). Only time under a work-based residence permit can be counted.

After the Exemption Period: How to Continue Working in Taiwan

Once your 2-year work permit exemption period comes to an end, if you plan to continue building your career in Taiwan, you’ll need to transition back to the formal employment system and apply for a work permit. At this stage, your job must fall under “specialized or technical work”, and your employer must meet certain requirements, such as minimum revenue or capital thresholds, as regulated by Taiwanese law. It’s highly recommended that you communicate with your employer in advance—before your exemption period expires—about your intention to stay. Your employer will typically assist with the application process, and you’ll need to prepare supporting documents together (such as your academic and professional credentials). Planning ahead is important to avoid delays that could affect your ability to work legally. Currently, there are two main pathways to apply for a work permit:

1.Points-Based System

This pathway is specifically designed for international and overseas Chinese graduates. Your eligibility is evaluated based on a points system, which includes factors such as, education level, salary, language proficiency, and work experience. If you score 70 points or above, your employer can apply for a work permit on your behalf. You are not subject to the minimum salary requirement of NT$47,971 under this system. However, your salary must still comply with Taiwan’s Basic Wage under the Labor Standards Act. This pathway still applies only to specialized or technical positions. (Click here to check more information regarding point-based applications)

2. Employment of Foreign Professionals Engaging in Specialized or Technical Works

If you do not apply through the points-based system, you can apply under the standard route for foreign professionals. In this case, you must meet: relevant educational background, work experience, or professional qualifications, AND the minimum monthly salary requirement of NT$47,971.

Important Reminder: Applying for a work permit involves administrative processing time, so early preparation is essential. Once your work permit exemption period expires, you are not allowed to continue working until your work permit has been officially approved. Working without approval may result in violations of Taiwanese regulations.

How Can International Graduates Start Preparing for Employment in Taiwan?

If you plan to build your career in Taiwan after graduation, it’s important to start preparing early. You can approach this from several aspects, including resume preparation, job research, skill development, and active job searching. Here are some practical steps to guide you:

1. Before graduation, obtain a TOCFL certification

Obtain a Test of Chinese as a Foreign Language (TOCFL) certificate (recommended at least B2 level, for the details refer to this article). TOCFL is Taiwan’s official Chinese proficiency test for non-native speakers, widely recognized for its reliability and credibility. It is advisable to obtain certification and clearly indicate your score on your resume as concrete proof of your language ability to your potential future employer.

2. Prepare and Optimize Your Resume

A clear and well-structured resume is the foundation of your job search.

International graduates can create an online resume on platforms like 104 Job Bank, making it easier for employers to find you and for you to apply to job openings. If you already have a resume, you can use the platform’s AI resume upload feature to organize and refine your content.

If you are unsure how to write a resume, you may refer to Resume-writing Guides or use 104 Resume Consultation Services to receive personalized improvement suggestions.

104 Resume Consultation Services: Get precise resume feedback from Industry Insiders in your target field

3. Actively Apply for Jobs

Take initiative in submitting applications and seizing opportunities. You can explore foreign-friendly job openings through platforms like the 104 Job Bank Foreign Talent section and apply directly.

4. Clearly Indicate Work Permit Status

When applying, clearly state your “no work permit required” status in your cover letter to reduce employers’ concerns.

5. Prepare for Interviews in Advance

Practice common interview components, including: self-introduction, past experiences, career goals and plans, etc.

Market Understanding and Preparation

1. Track Job Openings and Understand Employer Requirements
Follow target industries and companies (using the 104 Follow feature), and review job descriptions to understand required qualifications such as experience, education, skills, language ability, and certifications (e.g., TOEIC, Google Analytics). Also confirm required application materials (e.g., cover letter, portfolio).

2. Build Relevant Skills and Certifications Based on Job Requirements

Based on your target roles, actively develop the necessary skills or obtain certifications to enhance your competitiveness.

3. Understand Taiwan’s Workplace Culture

Familiarize yourself with local workplace expectations, such as: work attitude and professionalism, team collaboration, and communication styles. This will help you adapt more smoothly to the working environment in Taiwan.

If you’d like to further improve your job search skills—such as resume writing, interview preparation, or salary negotiation—you can refer to 104 Career+ (104職場力)’s “Complete Job Search Toolkit” for more guidance and resources.

2026 New Policy: Gain Practical Experience and Explore Your Career Path

The 2-year work permit exemption period is a crucial transition phase for international and overseas Chinese graduates. During this time, you can gradually build hands-on experience related to your career interests, gain deeper insights into Taiwan’s workplace culture and industries, and develop your professional knowledge and skills through real work experience.

At the same time, this period offers a high level of flexibility, allowing you to explore different fields, gain cross-disciplinary experience, and discover your long-term career direction. By actively participating in real work projects and job roles, you can also build your resume and portfolio, strengthen your language abilities and professional skills, and better prepare yourself for formal employment in the future.

As you continue gaining experience and building a strong working relationship with your employer, you can also start planning ahead for your next step—applying for a formal work permit under the existing system—laying a solid foundation for long-term career development in Taiwan.

Ready to take the first step in your career?
Visit the 104 Job Bank Foreign Talent section to explore a wide range of opportunities tailored for international talent—and start your journey in Taiwan today!

FAQ for International and Overseas Chinese Graduates

Q1: What is the “work permit exemption period”?

A: This is a new policy implemented in 2026. Eligible international and overseas Chinese graduates can work in Taiwan without a work permit for up to 2 years (or 1 year for Hong Kong and Macao graduates) after obtaining an “Extension of Stay”. During this period, employers are not required to apply for a work permit with the Ministry of Labor. However, employment must still comply with the Labor Standards Act, including the minimum wage requirement. As of January 1, 2026, the minimum monthly wage is NT$29,500.

Q2: Who is eligible for the work permit exemption?

A: Applicants must meet all of the following conditions:

  1. Be an overseas Chinese student or international student
  2. Have obtained at least an associate degree or higher from a Taiwanese college or university
  3. Currently hold an “Extension of Stay” status

This policy applies to graduates who remain in Taiwan for job-seeking under the Extension of Stay and have not yet entered formal employment. You are not eligible if you have already switched to a work-based residence permit, or have previously obtained a work permit and entered formal employment, then left your job.

Q3: I graduated in 2025 (ROC Year 114). Am I eligible for this new policy?

A: Yes—with conditions. This policy applies to graduates who stay in Taiwan under an “Extension of Stay” for job-seeking, within the 2-year period, and who have not yet entered formal employment. However, for graduates from ROC Year 114 (2025), their residence permits may not include the annotation “work permit exemption”. Starting from January 1, 2026, they can apply for a replacement ARC at local NIA service centers. Replacement fee is NT$500. However, the validity period of the Extension of Stay is still calculated based on the original issuance date of the first Extension of Stay permit, not the replacement date.

Q4: What kinds of work can I do during the Extension of Stay?

A: Very flexible! You can take on full-time, part-time (student jobs), internships, or project-based work, with no restriction on industry or job type. This period is an excellent opportunity to explore your career interests and gain practical work experience in Taiwan.

Q5: Do I have to apply for the full 2 years of Extension of Stay at once?

A: No. For international and overseas Chinese graduates, each Extension of Stay is issued for 1 year, and you must apply for an extension before it expires, up to a maximum of 2 years. For Hong Kong and Macao graduates, each Extension of Stay is issued for 6 months, up to a maximum of 1 year. Remember to always keep track of your residence permit expiration date.

Q6: Can the 2-year Extension of Stay period count toward the Permanent Resident (APRC) requirement?

A: No. Time spent under Extension of Stay does not count toward the continuous residence requirement for permanent residency. Only the period after obtaining a formal work permit and converting to work-based residence can be counted.

Q7: After the work permit exemption period ends, do I need a monthly salary above NT$47,971 to continue working?

A: Not necessarily. Your employer can apply for a work permit using the “points-based system”, which evaluates education, language skills, work experience, and other factors. If you reach 70 points, a work permit can be issued without meeting the NT$47,971 salary threshold, though the salary must still meet the Labor Standards Act minimum wage, which is NT$29,500 per month starting January 1, 2026. If you apply through the “Employment of Foreign Professionals Engaging in Specialized or Technical Works”, then the NT$47,971 monthly salary requirement applies.

Q8: If my work permit exemption period ends, but my new work permit hasn’t been approved yet, can I continue working?

A: No. Once your work permit exemption period expires, you cannot continue working until your formal work permit has been officially approved. It is recommended to discuss and submit the application with your employer 2–3 months before the exemption period ends to avoid any risk of working illegally. According to the Employment Service Act, engaging in illegal work may result in a fine of NT$30,000 to NT$150,000 for the individual.

Q9: During the work permit exemption period, can I have two jobs at the same time?

A: In principle, yes. As long as you hold a valid “Extended of Stay (ARC)” and are still within the work permit exemption period, Taiwanese law does not explicitly prohibit international graduates from holding more than one job. However, in practice, you should pay attention to the following points:

  • Employment must be legal:
    During the exemption period, you are allowed to take full-time, part-time (student work), internship, or project-based work, with no restrictions on industry or job type. All work must be under legal employment, and you cannot engage in illegal or undeclared work.
  • Working hours must comply with the Labor Standards Act:
    • Regular hours: Maximum 8 hours per day, 40 hours per week.
    • Overtime: Including regular hours, no more than 12 hours per day. Total monthly overtime must not exceed 46 hours.

Each employer must comply with these rules, so if you have multiple jobs, be careful to avoid excessive working hours.

  • Insurance and retirement contributions:
    If both jobs are considered employment relationships, your employers are generally required to handle insurance and pension contributions:
    • Labor Insurance: Both employers may need to enroll you.
    • National Health Insurance: Usually registered under your higher-paying main job.
    • Labor Pension: Employers must contribute according to the law.
      The actual coverage may vary depending on your work type and salary structure.
  • Check your employment contract and employer policies on secondary work:
    If you take multiple types of jobs (e.g., full-time + project work or part-time), confirm:
    • Any restrictions on secondary employment or non-compete clauses in your contract.
    • Potential conflicts of interest.
    • How income tax reporting is handled.

Tip: Communicate with your employers in advance and confirm the employment arrangements. This helps protect your rights and avoids potential legal or contractual disputes. If holding multiple jobs, also monitor your working hours, insurance enrollment, and tax reporting to ensure compliance with relevant regulations.

Q10: Is there a minimum salary requirement during the work permit exemption period?

A: During the work permit exemption period, the minimum salary threshold for “foreign professionals in specialized or technical roles” (NT$47,971) does not apply. However, you must still receive at least the minimum wage as set by the Labor Standards Act, which from January 1, 2026, is NT$29,500 per month, and the salary agreed upon with your employer. Therefore, employers are still legally required to pay no less than the minimum wage.

Q11: Do I need to file taxes during the work permit exemption period?

A: According to Taiwan’s tax regulations, whether you need to file taxes and the applicable tax rate depends on whether you qualify as a tax resident.

Reminder: Residency days are counted within the calendar year (Jan 1 – Dec 31). If you leave Taiwan early during the work permit exemption period and do not plan to return, you must complete the annual income tax filing before departure. For detailed guidance, refer to: Tax Filing Guide for Foreigners.

Q12: Can I change jobs during the work permit exemption period?

A: Yes. Under the work permit exemption system, you do not need to reapply for a work permit when changing jobs. This allows you to switch positions or explore different roles according to your career plan. However, you must still ensure that:

  1. Your residence permit remains valid.
  2. Your new job is legally compliant.
  3. Your labor contract with the previous employer is properly terminated or transitioned according to law.

Q13: What if my work permit exemption period ends but my work permit has not yet been approved?

A: You are not allowed to continue working if your exemption period has expired and your work permit is still pending approval. Working without approval may violate Taiwanese regulations. To avoid this, it is recommended to plan the application timeline with your employer in advance, ensuring that the permit approval process is completed before the exemption period ends.

Q14: Can I start a business or do freelance work during the work permit exemption period?

A: Under the latest regulations, graduates with a “Extension of Stay” residence permit can work in Taiwan without a separate work permit. This period is mainly intended to help graduates gain employment experience. Establishing a company or engaging in self-employment may involve other investment or business regulations. Before planning entrepreneurship, it is advisable to consult relevant authorities or professional advisors to confirm your eligibility under residence or investment rules. You can generally take on freelance projects. Practically, the cooperating organization may issue a service fee statement (“勞務報酬單”), from which income tax is withheld according to Taiwanese law. Freelance income must be declared in the annual comprehensive income tax filing (by May of the following year). The applicable withholding tax rate depends on whether you qualify as a tax resident (usually determined by staying in Taiwan for 183 days or more in a year).
